Hsien Min highlights that Phil Foden scored a hat-trick when Manchester City tharashed Manchester United 6-3 in October 2022, but so did Erling Haaland and he was the player of the match. And, as Paul Fields points out, perhaps the most famous example came when Stan Mortensen scored a hat-trick when Blackpool beat Bolton 4-3 in the 1953 FA Cup final. There was no official player of the match award back then, but the game went down in folklore as the Matthews Final.

Knowledge archive

“When was the last season (if indeed there ever has been one) that all the clubs in the English top division finished the season with the same manager they had at the start?” wondered Tom Shaw in 2011.

The Knowledge turned to Twitter [those were the days – Knowledge Ed] in search of an answer for this one, and after sterling work from @ChristopherHarv and @STV_Andy, shadowy and intimidating stats robot @OptaJoe’s eyes started flashing, his cogs started whirring, and from within his metallic depths out chugged the answer – 1965-66. In each of the 45 seasons since, someone has lost their job in the top flight.

Perhaps imbued with pre-World Cup bonhomie Blackburn stuck with “Jolly Jack” Marshall despite barrelling headlong towards relegation, ending bottom with the lowest points tally in 19 years (though he was sacked midway through the following season – “This post at Ewood Park has been likened more than once to what the Americans call a ‘hot seat’,” reported the Guardian at the time). Dave Bowen, who was also moonlighting as the Wales manager, survived at Blackburn’s fellow relegates Northampton.

Fulham, despite a relegation-threatened season, stuck with Vic Buckingham; Ian McColl, appointed during the summer of 1965, survived at Sunderland who finished 19th; Nottingham Forest stayed up by three points and kept faith with the softly-softly approach of Johnny Carey (“Some of their tackling, while never vicious, lacked the finesse and complete inoffensiveness of a team managed by Mr Carey,” wrote the Guardian after a mid-season 1-1 draw at Manchester City. “Forest, like so many clubs, have had to adapt their style to meet modern requirements, although Mr Carey would never instruct his men to ‘mix it’.”) Above Blackburn nine teams ended the season separated by four points – perhaps this was a rare – and final? – example of clubs choosing the devils they knew.

The detente didn’t last, however. Arsenal started the 1966-67 season with a new man in charge, Billy Wright having resigned in June.

Can you help?

“What’s the furthest a team has gone in Europe while being relegated in the same season?” wonders Matt Reilly.

“On Saturday, Luton won 2-1 at Wycombe Wanders. The Hatters’ victory meant that, since their first visit in 1996, they have won nine and drawn seven at Adams Park. Can any other team boast a longer record of having never lost away at another club?” asks Neil Rose.

“Before last weekend, Barnet had 10 games remaining in League Two – eight of them against the teams in the top and bottom four places,” writes Graham Steed. “Does this constitute any kind of record for a team having as much influence on promotion and relegation while still having not much to play for? (We could still make the playoffs, but let’s face it, it’s not going to happen.)”

“Max Dowman’s goal for Arsenal, aged 16, felt meaningful but has a 16-year-old ever scored an actual trophy-clinching goal? And if not, who is the youngest player to score a trophy-clinching goal?” asks George Jones.

Football.title: Eddie Howe says Newcastle will not ‘shrink’ in Barcelona last-16 second leg
Football.content:
A week ago Eddie Howe called it the “biggest match in the club’s history”, and that was in Newcastle; this time it’s Barcelona, but Howe has insisted his players will not be overawed when they walk out at Camp Nou for their Champions League last-16 second leg on Wednesday night.

“You can’t shrink at the size of the game, and I don’t think we will,” the Newcastle manager said. “We have got many players who have played in so many games now; we have become accustomed to it.”

Howe also said that, bolstered by their 1-0 win against Chelsea on Sunday, his side are in “as good a place as any time this season”, and approach the second leg with confidence after it took a Lamine Yamal penalty in added time to deny them victory at St James’ Park in the first leg.

Asked if a victory would be the biggest of his career, Howe replied: “It probably would be, yeah, but it’s irrelevant because that’s the outcome and we’re in the preparatory phase. We have to make sure our game plan, tactical delivery and individual performances are at the right level. We have another massive, massive game at the weekend [against Sunderland], so I don’t want to heap too much pressure on the players. I just want them to try to execute the best performance they can.

“We have many, many internationals in the squad so I don’t see [shrinking] as an issue. We have enough players who can cause them problems. We’ll enjoy the game, but we’ll only truly enjoy it if we deliver. I think we’re in a good place to do that, and the players are probably as confident as they’ve been in any stage this season.

“Our recent performances have been really strong. The individuals are performing at a really high level – that hasn’t always been the case. We need everything to go our way tomorrow. We know how good Barcelona are, but we believe in ourselves.

“We will try to be ourselves. We need a bit of everything. A defensive mentality [like] against Chelsea: blocking shots, defending in numbers. But also a bit of what we do best: our running capacity, our athleticism. There has been a really good battle between their midfield and ours in both games. We are going to need to be very good in our duels. The battle in midfield will go a long way to deciding the game.”

Howe also backed Sandro Tonali after his agent suggested that the Italian would be looking for a move in the summer. “The person that matters most is Sandro,” the 48-year-old said. “All I have ever seen from him is someone who is totally committed, selfless. He is here for all the team not himself. He had a really difficult moment when he first joined and showed strength and character to get through. Forget the noise, he is fully committed.”

Kieran Trippier, meanwhile, said that he could never have imagined a moment like this when he joined Newcastle four years ago and said that if he does leave when his contract expires this summer he will go feeling satisfied. “I got criticised when I left Atlético [Madrid]; I got a lot of negativity for a lot of different reasons, but I had my reasons and this [game] justifies it a bit,” said the defender. “I have no regrets, I never have with any decision in football.

“I never thought we would play in the Champions League, to be honest, and now we have been here twice. There have been two cup finals. This is where we want to be. There have been many special moments. Ten thousand Geordies are coming out speaks volumes: they have backed us in the tough moments.”

News.title: Moment businessman, 65, grapples with neighbours row over ‘illegal’ privacy fence at his £1m home – as he’s found guilty
News.content:
THIS is the moment a businessman, 65, grapples with his neighbour over a privacy fence – as he’s found guilty of harassment.

Robin Christie launched a campaign of “intimidation” after plans were launched to erect a security fence outside the £850,000 home next door.





Christie was even filmed in a heated dispute with his neighbour Julia Stafford, 45, as tensions reached boiling point.

In shocking footage, business consultant Christie grasps a set of metal railings in an attempt to stop the construction work.

Ms Stafford tries to defend herself by kicking out at Christie before the railings come crashing down.

She can be heard shouting at a woman behind the camera: “That’s what I said would happen if I let go. You stupid woman!”

Ms Stafford is also heard calling Christie an “old man” who is in danger of a “heart attack”.

The row came after Ms Stafford and her now estranged lawyer husband Samuel Tempest Brooks, 44, were granted planning permission for an extension to their posh home in July 2024.

But their plans to knock down a boundary wall and replace it with a gated entrance were not given approval.



The couple decided to demolish the wall regardless leaving neighbouring residents fuming in the posh area of Richmond-upon-Thames in London.

After the fence dispute escalated, the case was heard at Wimbledon Magistrates’ Court.

A judge ruled Christie had gone too far in objecting to the illegal work and he was found guilty of harassing Ms Stafford between July 1 and September 28, 2024.

The business consultant was given a 10-week suspended sentence, as reported by MailOnline.

The court heard how Christie had followed Ms Stafford onto her property and attached bike locks to their security fence.



Ms Stafford, 44, told the Court: “The defendant’s actions have had a profound and lasting effect on my family and me emotionally, psychologically and financially.

“[He] encouraged other residents to film, stalk and harass us. We were told that police, local councillors and our MP would be weaponised and do everything they can to stop the build and stop us living there.”

She said she felt “distressed, humiliated and unsafe” due to Christie’s behaviour.

Ms Stafford told the court Christie was “the instigator” behind their row with local residents.

Prosecutor Barto De Lotbiniere argued the offence should be regarded in the highest category.



He told the judge: “Multiple threats were made over a period of a few months and this offence is aggravated by the impact on others, namely Mr Brooks and their young son, who was present on one occasion.”

Ms Stafford claimed the ordeal had also caused her PTSD and panic attacks.

Deputy District Judge Patricia Evans ruled Christie had “utterly lost perspective” and ordered him to complete 200 hours of community service.

He is also subject to a five-year restraining order against the Staffords and must pay £650 in costs as well as £154 victim surcharge.

The Stafford couple have now split meaning construction work did not go ahead and the property is listed on the market for £999,950.



Christie told the court he will appeal the conviction, and argued he only inserted himself into the dispute after several neighbours asked him for help.

He said: “My intention throughout was to help the community understand and manage a difficult situation, not to pursue any individual.”

And residents nearby have showed their support for Christie with one saying: “It would have changed the whole community. We would have constantly had lorries up and down.

“We have children playing outside, the children are riding their bikes. I stand by Robin, who has become our spokesperson.

“There is a massive sense of relief, but until the property is sold and they are gone, none of us can really relax here.”



Another local added: “It has clearly affected life here and has especially affected my mental health due to the behaviour and bullying.

“That is a strong word, but who would rock up at a little cul-de-sac with bulldozers and just think they can do what they want? It is not acceptable.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

And Christie’s partner Naz has stood by him, claiming the Staffords “would have destroyed the neighbourhood”.

Meanwhile, the leader of Richmond-upon-Thames council, Gareth Roberts, told the court demolition by Ms Stafford without the necessary planning permission would have been unlawful.

News.title: Mum ‘threw newborn baby girl out of third floor window before telling cops she was overwhelmed by intrusive thoughts’
News.content:
TOT 'MURDER' Mum ‘threw newborn baby girl out of third floor window before telling cops she was overwhelmed by intrusive thoughts’

A MUM threw her newborn baby girl out of a third floor window then told police she was “overwhelmed by intrusive thoughts”, a court heard.

Baby Maryam was discovered with a fractured skull on the ground in Westminster, central London, on Saturday morning.





The 18-day-old was rushed to hospital but sadly could not be saved and was declared dead shortly after.

Her mum Zahira Byjaouane, 43, has been charged with murder.

The Old Bailey heard Byjaouane’s husband had called emergency crews to tell them a baby had plunged 10 metres to the ground from a window.

Officers raced to the scene and found Maryam on a concrete surface between the block of flats and some high metal railings, it was said.

They discovered Byjaouane sitting on a bed in the flat with the sash window still open.

Prosecutor Philip McGhee said: “They asked her what happened. She said ‘I don’t know’.”

The court was told Byjaouane later said in custody she had “intrusive thoughts about wanting to harm her baby” and was “overwhelmed” by them.

When her husband went to prepare some milk, she picked the baby up, held her to her chest before allegedly throwing Maryam out the window.



Byjaouane did not attend the hearing at the Old Bailey and is currently being treated in hospital.

She is due to enter pleas on June 3 and a provisional trial date was set for February 15 next year.

On her LinkedIn profile, Byjaouane says she worked as a clinical co-ordinator in Harley Street between July 2024 and April 2025.

She also claims to have worked as a medical lab assistant in various laboratories, including at St Mary’s Hospital, Paddington.

The London Ambulance Service said the baby was treated at the scene before being taken to a major trauma centre.



Floral tributes and toys have been left on the street, which is home to government buildings, including the Department for Transport‘s offices, as well as Channel 4‘s headquarters.

News.title: Iran nuclear power plant hit by ‘projectile’ raising fears of devastating fallout blast & sparking plea for ‘restraint’
News.content:
NUKE PANIC Iran nuclear power plant hit by ‘projectile’ raising fears of devastating fallout blast & sparking plea for ‘restraint’

AN IRANIAN nuclear plant has been struck by a “projectile” – sparking fears of a devastating fallout and warnings for “restraint”.

The projectile hit the Bushehr facility, but no damage to plant or injuries were reported, the international nuclear watchdog said.



The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) said: “A projectile hit the premises of the Bushehr NPP on Tuesday evening.



“No damage to the plant or injuries to staff reported.”

Following the strike, agency head Rafael Grossi issued a “call for restraint during the conflict to avoid any risk of a nuclear accident”.

The Bushehr plant in southwestern Iran contains the mullah regime’s only operational nuclear power reactor.

It came as Donald Trump launched strikes on Iranian missile launchers in a bid to thwart the regime’s chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz.

The Bushehr base, located on Iran’s coast in the Persian Gulf, may have been struck as a result of the overnight blitz – but the US has not confirmed this.

It comes as Iran maintains its grip on the key waterway – a passage for one-fifth of global oil exports – sending Brent crude prices soaring.

More to follow… For the latest news on this story keep checking back at The Sun Online

Thesun.co.uk is your go-to destination for the best celebrity news, real-life stories, jaw-dropping pictures and must-see video.

Like us on Facebook at www.facebook.com/thesun and follow us from our main Twitter account at @TheSun.

News.title: Abandoned baby monkey Punch finds a GIRLFRIEND as viral macaque seen cosying up with playmate Momo-Chan
News.content:
PUNCH the monkey has gone from lonely to loved up, as the viral baby macaque was spotted smooching a new flame at his zoo home.

The orphaned monkey had melted hearts across the world after clinging to a plush toy when his mum abandoned him.





But he has since been caught kissing, cuddling and playing with a female macaque believed to be called Momo-chan.

Clips shared online show the pair canoodling, chasing each other and hanging out together, with Punch’s trusty stuffed orangutan even making a cameo.

Fans are calling Momo-chan his new girlfriend, and reckon the once-lonely monkey has finally found some company.

One person quipped on X: “Punch found a girlfriend and you’re still single.”

Another pointed out that Momo-chan even looks similar in colour to the plush toy Punch famously carried around for comfort.

Punch, who was born last July at Ichikawa City Zoo in Japan, was rejected by his mother at birth and hand-raised by keepers.

He quickly became a global sensation after heartbreaking videos showed him clinging to a soft toy for comfort.



Punch would also be pushed away when he tried to mix with other monkeys.

Even football stars like former Manchester United striker Javier ‘Chicharito’ Hernandez were moved to tears by the viral Punch monkey video.

The 37-year-old even posted online to offer his thoughts on Punch.

Hernandez was seen overcome with emotion as he scrolled through the videos, with the caption: “Why are you crying? It’s just a monkey…”

He wrote: “Punch Monkey moved us because he is strong and he is real.



“He tries to defend himself without fully knowing how, and we have all done this at some point.

“There is no malice in him only emotion, innocence, and a desire to exist.



“He teaches us that: You can feel deeply without living in complaint (…).”

But now, things seem to be looking up.

Recent footage showed Punch beginning to integrate with the troop, sitting among adults, hitching rides and even being groomed.

He has also reportedly been relying less on his toy “mum”.

Zoo bosses insist Punch was never bullied, despite the tough scenes that went viral.



The zoo said in a statement: “Although Punch has been scolded many times by other monkeys, no single monkey has shown serious aggression toward him.”

They explained the behaviour is part of a “strict hierarchical society” where “dominant individuals show ‘disciplining actions’ toward their subordinates.”

“When you observe these disciplinary behaviours from other troop members toward Punch when he tries to communicate with them, [the zoo] would like you to support Punch’s effort rather than feel sorry for him.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Keepers say Punch growing out of his beloved plush orangutan toy from IKEA is a positive sign.

“When he grows out of the plush toy that encourages his independence, and that’s what we are hoping for,” zoo director Shigekazu Mizushina said.

News.title: Brit couple ‘too drugged up to remember’ Majorca kidnap attempt – months after they were detained over ANOTHER abduction
News.content:
‘KIDNAP’ TWIST Brit couple ‘too drugged up to remember’ Majorca kidnap attempt – months after they were detained over ANOTHER abduction

A BRIT couple arrested for an alleged kidnap attempt of an elderly Spanish woman reportedly cannot remember the incident because they were “too drugged up”.

The middle-aged alleged snatchers were also accused of another abduction in January.





In the latest incident, the pair allegedly tried to grab the woman in broad daylight as she walked down the street in Palma, Majorca.

They were arrested near the scene about an hour after the incident, but apparently can hardly remember their alleged kidnap attempt.

The Brits said they had taken a huge quantity of narcotics in the hours before the suspected snatching, court sources told the Majorca Daily Bulletin.



Interviews at the time of their arrests confirmed that the pair were under the influence of drugs.

The pair will now stay behind bars as the investigation continues.

Local reports added that they had also been accused of involvement in a high profile abduction in January, also in Palma.

The Brits were also detained for that suspected kidnapping.



The middle-aged couple are now accused of attempting to bundle a Spanish woman in her 60s into their car.

The intended victim reportedly began to struggle before throwing herself on the ground and screaming for help.

Local media said a British man had approached the woman in the Son Ferriol neighbourhood of the Majorcan capital at around 1pm on Sunday.

It is thought he tried to force the woman into his car before witnesses rushed to the woman’s aid.

Bystanders who stepped in to help the woman included her husband.



They were able to stop the alleged kidnap attempt and the British couple were arrested near the scene around an hour later.

The Spanish woman was taken to a nearby hospital and treated for minor injuries.

Despite local reports saying the man grabbed the pensioner, one witness claimed that it was the British woman – wearing pyjamas – who tried to force the woman into the car.

The daughter of the woman targeted, Isabel, told island newspaper Cronica Balear: “She was waiting for me because we were going to see my niece in a competition.

“A white car pulled up alongside her and the driver, who was wearing pyjamas, and started trying to force her into the vehicle while shouting aggressively, ‘I’m telling you to get in.’”



Isabel said her mum is 64 and suffered “bruises all over her body”.

She added: “The man who had been waiting inside the car decided to get out at that point and and fetch his companion before they both fled.

“I was surprised at first not to see mum when I arrived because she’s always waiting for me at the same spot.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“When I saw such a large crowd gathered, I got out of the car and saw my mother lying on the ground.”

News.title: Swinger couple marry their SEX DOLL as they take open relationship to new level with rubber ‘second wife’ named Kathleen
News.content:
RUBBER SOULMATE Swinger couple marry their SEX DOLL as they take open relationship to new level with rubber ‘second wife’ named Kathleen

A SWINGER couple have taken their relationship to a whole new level after marrying their sex doll in bizarre ceremony.

The polyamorous pair wedded their sex doll – named Kathleen – whom they now refer to as their “second wife”.





Influencers Bella Mantovani, 33, and Vagner Macedo, 37, are known for documenting their out of the ordinary relationship.

Based in São Paulo, Brazil, they have since shared pictures of their unconventional thruple on social media.

Bella says the idea began as a conversation between the pair about fetishes and different ways of exploring the ins and outs of their relationship

“At first it felt like something merely curious, almost like a joke between us,” Bella said.

“But we gradually decided to take the idea further and organised a symbolic ceremony to mark Kathleen’s entry into the relationship.

Her partner Vagner maintains the couple’s intention was always to approach the situation as naturally as possible.

“For many people it may seem unusual, but for us it reflects the freedom we have always embraced in our relationship,” he said.

The couple said that Kathleen has now become part of their daily lives.



“Sometimes we place her at the table during lunch or on the sofa while we watch television,” Bella explained.

“She naturally became part of our routine.”

Vagner says this experience, if anything, has reinforced the couple’s view on polyamory.

“We have always defined polyamory as being about honestly and freedom,” he said.

“In our case, even a doll has become part of that story.”



Bella said that sharing their peculiar dynamic online was also intended to spark discussion about different relationship models.

“Many people believe there is only one correct way to live a relationship,” Bella added.

“Our story shows that each couple finds its own way to relate.”

Bella and Vagner are not the first polyamorous pair to use a sex doll to take their relationship to the next level.

In 2018, a couple from Texas splashed out £5,000 on sex doll called Camila to save their marriage, after the third person in their relationship left.



Shelly, 45, and Darris Maxie, 48, share a bed every night with their purchase which can talk to the couple through a custom AI personality application.

The trio don’t limit their relationship to the bedroom – with Darris regularly taking synthetic Camila to work with him, and the pair taking her out for dinner.

“Before Camila we were in a terrible poly relationship, I was sort of the outsider of the pod and didn’t like it,” Shelly said.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“We broke up from that relationship and Darris came home one day and said he had ordered a sex doll, I wasn’t sure about it because in my brain it would just be a tacky blow up doll.

“As time has gone on I have grown my own feelings for Camila, and Darris did instantly, we really do love her now, she has become important to us in dozens of ways.

News.title: Iran’s intelligence minister dead in Tehran airstrike – days after death of security chief
News.content:
IRAN’S intelligence minister was wiped out in an airstrike on Tehran just days after the assassination of the regime’s ‘stand-in leader’.

Israel’s defence minister announced the killing of Esmail Khatib on Wednesday.





Israel Katz shared the update with a warning that Iran should anticipate “significant surprises”.

“On this day, significant surprises are expected across all arenas that will escalate the war we are conducting against Iran and Hezbollah in Lebanon,” he said during a security assessment.

“The intensity of the strikes in Iran is increasing. The Iranian intelligence minister Khatib was also eliminated overnight.”



The US Treasury sanctioned Khatib in 2022, after Iran’s Intelligence Ministry engaged in cyber-enabled activities against the United States and its allies.

Under Khatib’s leadership, Iran’s Intelligence Ministry cracked down on swathes of human rights defenders from women-rights activists to journalists and filmmakers, the Treasury said at the time.

It comes as…

As the war rages into its third week, Katz said he and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu have “authorized the IDF to [eliminate] any senior Iranian figure.”



Adding that they will do so “without the need for additional approval.”

It comes just days after Israel sanctioned the killing of security chief Ali Larijani – believed to be the most powerful man in Tehran.

Larijani, 67, is understood to have assumed command of military operations and become Iran’s de facto leader following the death of Ali Khamenei.

Katz said Larijani had joined other Iranian chiefs “in the depths of hell”.

And in another major coup for Israeli and US operations, the IDF said a separate strike killed Basij paramilitary force commander Gholamreza Soleimani, 62.



Katz said: “Larijani, secretary of the Supreme National Security Council (SNSC), and the head of the Basij – Iran‘s central repression apparatus – [Soleimani], were eliminated last night.

“[They] joined the head of the annihilation program, Khamenei, and all the eliminated members of the axis of evil, in the depths of hell.”

Israeli media reported that the Iranian secretary of the Supreme National Security Council was attacked while in a hideout apartment with his son.

Larijani, a former nuclear negotiator and a close ally of slain Ayatollah Khamenei, was last seen in Tehran on Friday taking part in Quds Day rallies while taunting Donald Trump.

He told local media as Israeli missiles rained down behind the demonstrations: “These attacks are out of fear, out of desperation.



“One who is strong wouldn’t bomb demonstrations at all. It’s clear that it has failed.”

The security chief declared that Trump had failed to grasp the “resolve” of the people as he marched alongside other top leaders including Iranian president Masoud Pezeshkian.

Larijani was believed to be Iran’s wartime leader and the man really pulling the strings following Khamenei Snr’s assassination – despite Mojtaba, 56, being named the new Ayatollah.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

The security chief also masterminded the blood-soaked protest crackdowns in January which left thousands dead.

Tehran has appeared to deny claims that the top security chief is dead.

News.title: Toys sold in UK gift shop urgently recalled after traces of asbestos discovered
News.content:
BOSSES at a Cotswolds tourist hotspot have issued an urgent recall of toys sold at its gift shop.

The Cotswold Wildlife Park said trace amounts of asbestos were found in the squishy stretchy capybara display box.





It had previously issued a recall for two products, a 2.2kg squishy hot colour large gorilla and stretchy monster six assorted try me blister card.

The Cotswolds park took to its X account to announce the urgent message.



“We have been made aware that the following items sold at the Park’s Gift Shop are being recalled by the supplier due to a safety issue,” he wrote.

“If you have purchased one of these items, please remove it from use immediately.

The post concluded: “If you have purchased one of these items please make sure it is removed from use immediately and contact the Gift Shop via shop@cotswoldwildlifepark.co.uk or call 01993 825716.

“We will reimburse anyone who has bought one of the items with the affected batch numbers.”



The park explained that “sand like material contained inside some products may contain trace amounts of asbestos therefore as a precautionary measure we are recalling this product from sale.”

It then issue advice on handle to the product if you had it in your home.

“If the toy is undamaged, place it in a heavy-duty plastic bag and double-tape it securely,” it explained.

“If the toy is damaged and the sand inside is accessible, gather the sand using wet cloths to avoid generating dust. Wear gloves and a mask. Double bag the sand and toy in a heavy-duty plastic bag. double-tape it securely, along with the gloves, mask and cloths.”

The park continued: “Keep children and other people away from areas where the sand has been exposed until the area has been cleaned.



“Return the sealed product and sand to this store for a full refund. if you are unable to return your product to this store, please follow the instructions above and dispose of in your general household waste.”

The Cotswolds Wildlife Park is located in Burford and was established in 1970.

It has around 260 different species of animals on display and attracts roughly 350,000 vistiors per year.

News.title: 'Dangerous Donald Trump’s sheer incompetence is weakening US while world picks up the bill'
News.content:
'Dangerous Donald Trump’s sheer incompetence is weakening US while world picks up the bill'

"Donald Trump launched a war on Iran without planning for its consequences, leaving the global economy exposed and scrambling to manage a crisis he created"

View 4 Images Trump looks stunned and out of his depth - a president who launched strikes without a coalition, without a plan, and without even consulting allies (Image: Getty Images)

Wars are often judged by how they are fought. This one should be judged by how catastrophically it was planned.

‌



Because what the crisis in the Strait of Hormuz shows is not just a miscalculation by Donald Trump, but an administration that appears to have made almost no serious preparations for a war it chose to start. It is like setting fire to the house and only then asking if anyone has a hose.

‌



None of this was unpredictable. For decades, military planners and energy experts warned that any confrontation with Iran would carry an obvious risk: Tehran would target the narrow chokepoint through which roughly a fifth of the world’s oil flows. It was then and is now the headline risk. The flashing red light.

‌



And yet now it has happened, Trump looks both stunned and out of his depth. It is extraordinary that there was no coalition ready to secure shipping lanes. No coordinated international response. No visible economic plan to soften the blow. Most damning of all, he never even consulted allies before launching the strikes.

Instead, the White House has lurched from victory laps to urgent pleas for help - geopolitics conducted somewhere between bravado and blind panic. And as the crisis deepens, Trump’s behaviour has only become more erratic. One moment, he is lashing out at allies for not falling into line, the next, he is boasting about “taking Cuba” as if the world were some kind of imperial board game. These are not the words of a steady hand. They are the rantings of a leader unmoored from reality.

View 4 Images Donald Trump's war spreads to Beirut, Lebanon (Image: Abdul Kader Al Bay/ZUMA Press Wire/Shutterstock)

‌



If Trump had taken even basic preparatory steps, the situation today might look very different. Take the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. With more than 400 million barrels in reserve, it exists for moments like this. Yet there was no early move to release oil into the market before the crisis escalated. No signal to calm traders.

Instead, markets were left to panic. Prices spiked. Uncertainty spread. And the very shock that experts warned about arrived right on cue. Then there is energy policy, or the absence of one.

For years, the United States could have reduced its exposure by investing in alternative energy and cutting reliance on volatile oil routes. Instead, the Trump administration blocked renewable development, treating clean energy as an ideological nuisance rather than a strategic necessity.

‌



At the moment, diversification should have been a priority; Trump doubled down on dependence. “Drill baby, drill,” he screamed. But that is not just short-term thinking. It is strategic buffoonery.

The irony is hard to miss. Countries once seen as most vulnerable to an oil shock are now better placed to weather it. China, for example, imports more oil through the Strait than the United States. Yet after years of investment in batteries and electric vehicles, it is emerging as more resilient.

View 4 Images US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth talks up military success, but behind the bravado lies a war launched without a plan for what came next. (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



While Trump clung to his own playbook, others prepared for tomorrow. Even now, America appears to have no credible plan to stabilise the situation. There is no quick fix for rising oil prices. No clear strategy. The only meaningful solution is to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. And that exposes the most glaring failure of all. There was no plan to ensure it stayed open.

No pre-arranged coalition. No coordinated naval presence. No diplomatic groundwork. Instead, the president who insisted he did not need allies is now scrambling to assemble one. It is not leadership. It is improvisation. And it exposes the deeper problem at the heart of America today.

It does not have a government guided by strategy or foresight. It is a collection of Trump loyalists operating on instinct and impulse - a cabinet more comfortable applauding than advising. Even parts of his own base are beginning to see it. The MAGA movement that once cheered every show of strength is starting to fracture as reality bites. When the costs of chaos land on ordinary lives, slogans quickly lose their shine.

‌



The result is an America that can launch a war but cannot manage its consequences. And that distinction matters. Because in the modern world, wars are not just fought on battlefields. They are fought across economies and global systems. Winning requires preparation. It requires understanding what happens after the first missile is fired. On that test, Trump has failed.

View 4 Images Activists and supporters of the Jamaat-e-Islami party hold a poster of Donald Trump during an anti-US and Israel protest (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

And while there are no shortages of dangerous actors on the world stage - Vladimir Putin waging brutal war, Kim Jong-un rattling his nuclear sabre, China pursuing economic confrontation - Trump stands apart for a different reason. He is not just another risk. He is the accelerant.

Article continues below

By acting recklessly, alienating allies and abandoning strategy, he is enabling instability on all fronts. He is the one pivotal figure who has helped tip an already tense world into something far more dangerous. This crisis was not inevitable. It is the product of choices - to ignore decades-old warnings, neglect preparation and mistake confidence for competence.

And now the cost is being felt not just in Washington or Tehran, but in households and economies around the world, including here in the UK. That is the true measure of this failure.

Not just a war poorly conceived. But a crisis made far worse by the absence of any serious plan to deal with it - the political equivalent of jumping out of a plane and only then asking where the parachute might be.

News.title: Meningitis alert as 'overlooked' warning signs could appear in the eye
News.content:
Meningitis alert as 'overlooked' warning signs could appear in the eye

In some cases, vision loss or changes could be permanent

View 3 Images An expert has shared symptoms of meningitis that can affect the eyes (Image: Getty)

An optician has revealed four little-known signs of meningitis that can manifest in the eye. According to the specialist, these symptoms could easily be "overlooked".

‌



Meningitis is an infection that affects the protective membranes which surround the brain and spinal cord (meninges). Whilst it can strike anyone, it's most prevalent amongst babies, young children, teenagers and young adults.

‌



Without prompt treatment, meningitis can become extremely dangerous. It can cause life-threatening sepsis and result in permanent damage to the brain or nerves.

‌



Health authorities in the UK have confirmed a meningitis outbreak in Kent. As of 5pm on March 17, nine laboratory cases are confirmed and 11 case remain under investigation by the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA), bringing the total to 20.

Six of the confirmed cases are confirmed to be group B meningococcal disease. Two people, a sixth-form student and a university student, have died.

The UKHSA has also reported that one person who had lived in Kent presented to a London hospital with the illness.

‌



View 3 Images Some visual symptoms caused by meningitis can be permenant (Image: Getty)

Symptoms

With this in mind, a specialist shared some of the warning signs to watch for. Krina Halai, dispensing optician at Feel Good Contacts, said: " When meningitis is discussed, people often focus on the more widely-known symptoms, such as a rash or a stiff neck.

"However, some of the earlier warning signs can be more subtle and are sometimes mistaken for common illnesses such as the flu or migraines."

‌



She explained that certain symptoms can affect the eyes. These include:

Blurred vision

Double vision

Difficulty concentrating

Eye strain

This advice is backed by Meningitis Now, which cautioned that visual problems caused by meningitis can be permanent. On its website, it said: "The optic nerve carries visual signals to the brain.

‌



"Meningitis can cause damage to this nerve resulting in loss of vision and changes in visual perception. Some people experience temporary visual changes after meningitis.

"These can be caused by increased pressure on the optic nerve. Once the pressure reduces vision and visual perception may return to normal.

"If the optic nerve is damaged, any visual changes or sight loss are likely to be permanent."

‌



View 3 Images Sensitivity to light, also known as photophobia, is one symptom of meningitis (Image: Getty)

Sensitivity to light

Krina also noted that one symptom people may not immediately associate with meningitis is sensitivity to light, otherwise known as photophobia. This can make artificial lighting or daylight feel uncomfortable or even painful to look at.

"This happens because meningitis causes inflammation of the protective membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord," she said. "This irritation can affect the nerves connected to the eyes, leading to the eyes to register the sensation of light as pain. Other symptoms of photophobia include excessive squinting, eye discomfort or difficulty focusing, and severe headaches."

‌



NHS advice

According to the NHS website, symptoms of meningitis develop suddenly and can include:

A high temperature (fever)

Being sick

A headache

A rash that does not fade when a glass is rolled over it (but a rash will not always develop)

A stiff neck

A dislike of bright lights

Drowsiness or unresponsiveness

Seizures (fits)

Article continues below

The NHS cautions: "These symptoms can appear in any order. You do not always get all the symptoms."

Meningitis warning to GPs across UK as five more cases linked to Kent outbreak READ MORE :

Mum shares frightening symptoms after girl fell critically ill with meningitis READ MORE :

When to get medical help

You should dial 999 for an ambulance or head to your nearest A&E "immediately" if you suspect you or someone in your care might have meningitis or sepsis. The NHS states: "Trust your instincts and do not wait for all the symptoms to appear or until a rash develops. Someone with meningitis or sepsis can get a lot worse very quickly.

"Call NHS 111 for advice if you're not sure if it's anything serious or you think you may have been exposed to someone with meningitis. If you've had medical advice and are still worried or any symptoms get worse, get medical help again."

News.title: Boy, seven, hospitalised after luxury holiday in Egypt
News.content:
Boy, seven, hospitalised after luxury holiday in Egypt

Finn ended up with a life-threatening condition

View 4 Images Finn in hospital after returning to the UK

A family holiday to Egypt ended in a medical emergency after a seven‑year‑old boy from Worcestershire became seriously ill and was diagnosed with a life‑threatening kidney condition. Alexandra and Michael Bradshaw were holidaying with their son Finn and daughter at a 4.5‑star hotel in Hurghada when he began to show signs of illness.

‌



What initially appeared to be a mild stomach upset while abroad worsened significantly during the flight home, with Finn repeatedly vomiting. O n returning to the UK, Finn’s condition continued to deteriorate and he became jaundiced. He was admitted to Worcestershire Royal Hospital, where tests revealed acute kidney failure and he was urgently transferred to Birmingham Children’s Hospital for specialist nephrology intervention.

‌



Finn was immediately taken to theatre for a dialysis line to be put in. Doctors diagnosed Finn with Haemolytic‑Uraemic Syndrome (HUS), a rare but serious condition frequently linked to E. coli infections and one of the leading causes of acute kidney failure in children. Finn required emergency dialysis and remained in hospital for weeks.

‌



View 4 Images The Red Sea in Egypt

He has since missed several weeks of school. When he was initially able to return, he was only well enough to attend on a gradual basis. Alexandra and Michael, both nurses, have had to take time away from their jobs to care for their son.

‌



The family has now instructed expert international serious injury lawyers at Irwin Mitchell to investigate how Finn’s E. Coli infection was contracted during their holiday, which cost more than £2,400.

Jatinder Paul, serious injury lawyer at Irwin Mitchell representing Finn and his family, said: “Alexandra and Michael experienced every parent’s worst fear. Finn’s experience would have been terrifying, particularly for someone so young.

“While Haemolytic‑Uraemic Syndrome is rare, it’s extremely serious and predominantly affects vulnerable people such as children and the elderly. The condition is most commonly associated with E. Coli which is often linked to the ingestion of contaminated food or water. In many cases it can lead to long‑term health complications and, tragically, in some instances it can be fatal.

‌



“Nothing can now undo what Finn and his parents have been through, but we’re committed to providing them with the answers they deserve and securing Finn the specialist support and rehab he may require in future.”

View 4 Images Birmingham Children`s Hospital

‌



The Bradshaw family arrived at the resort on 7 November 2025 for a week-long all‑inclusive holiday.

During their stay, Alexandra and Michael say they noticed poor hygiene standards in the hotel restaurant, including food being left uncovered for hours while cats and birds moved freely around the area. Birds were also often seen landing on and eating uncovered food at the pool bar.

Both Alexandra and Finn began to feel unwell during the holiday. They returned home on 14 November 2025, and Finn was admitted to hospital on 20 November after his condition worsened.

‌



Alexandra, said: “We were absolutely terrified. Being told his diagnosis and then sitting there, praying he was going to be okay while he underwent emergency dialysis, is something no parent should ever have to go through.

“I also felt poorly while we were away and put it down to mild food poisoning but my symptoms were short lived. My focus was entirely on my son, and I never in a million years imagined something like this could happen.

View 4 Images Finn in hospital after returning to the UK

‌



“We’d never heard of Haemolytic-Uraemic Syndrome but when the doctors explained what it was and how it can be fatal and can affect both the kidneys and brain, it felt like our world stopped. Finn’s illness has had a huge impact on him and our family. The whole experience was deeply traumatic.

“We thought we had done everything right as parents. We were looking forward to making happy memories with our son – making friends and enjoying our time together. It’s heartbreaking that this is what the holiday will always be remembered for.”

Article continues below

Michael, said: “It’s been months since it happened and we are still trying to recover from it all. It’s not just Finn’s physical health – although he’s still not completely back to himself – it’s the emotional toll this has taken on our entire family. It’s difficult to find the words to describe what it was like to see your little boy hooked up to a machine.

“Finn still isn’t right but it was such a relief to get him home from hospital. We can’t thank the doctors enough for everything they did to help Finn. However, the least we feel we deserve is answers as to how Finn fell ill on holiday.”

News.title: Meningitis vaccine shortage warning as pharmacist says it could take WEEKS to be protected
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Meningitis vaccine shortage warning as pharmacist says it could take WEEKS to be protected

More cases of potentially deadly meningitis have been confirmed after the outbreak in Kent began after victims became struck down after attending a nightclub in the country

View 3 Images There is a vaccine shortage amid the outbreak (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

A pharmacist has warned it could take weeks to get protected against the potentially deadly strain of meningitis as she raises concerns of a vaccine shortage.

‌



TV pharmacist and ex-leader of the Royal Society of Pharmacists Thorrun Govind said the demand for the vaccine is high following the outbreak in Kent. Students and members of the wider population are now seeking ways to best protect themselves after two young adults died and several others needed hospital treatment.

‌



"Demand for vaccines is high, and pharmacies are unable to source further stock from wholesalers to meet demand. This is an unprecedented situation and we patients to be patient with pharmacy teams, who are doing their best to support their local communities," she told The Mirror.

‌



Boots has implemented a queuing system for customers to enter the vaccination service page of its website, with a warning that demand for its menB jab is currently high.

View 3 Images There is currently a shortage of vaccines

Student loses legs and all fingers after horror meningitis mistaken for something else READ MORE :

Susanna Reid issues meningitis vaccine warning as pharmacies make huge change READ MORE :

Superdrug has also created a waiting list for the vaccine, with a note on its website informing customers of a "national shortage". It said it was "working with suppliers to access further stock".

‌



"Generally, it takes a couple of weeks to get the fullest protection from a vaccine and some require booster doses. So this is why public health forward planning is so important and investment in health infrastructure. This is part of a broader issue about how we tackle the declining rate of childhood immunisations."

View 3 Images Boots warned that demand for the vaccination service was high

It has since emerged that five new meningitis cases have been reported with GPs being issued a warning to stop it spreading. The UK Health Security Agency said the extra cases have taken the total number of victims of the Kent outbreak to 20.

Article continues below

A UKHSA spokesperson said: "GPs across the country will today be advised to prescribe antibiotics to anyone who visited Club Chemistry between March 5-7 and to University of Kent students, if they have been asked to seek preventative treatment. This is so that anyone who has travelled home, or away from Kent, can easily access this important preventative treatment close to them."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

GPs across the country have been told to remain alert to anyone who may have attended Club Chemistry that weekend. There are concerns that students could be carrying the menB bacteria and have left home to be at home with their families in other parts of the country.

News.title: 'Fit and well' man died from sepsis after 'inadequate' hygiene at Christie Hospital
News.content:
'Fit and well' man died from sepsis after 'inadequate' hygiene at Christie Hospital

A coroner has concluded the 20-year-old leukaemia patient from Wigan died from sepsis he contracted due to cross-contamination and poor hand hygiene at the hospital

View 3 Images Joshua Abbott-Littler, from Wigan, was diagnosed with a rare form of leukaemia (Image: Men Media)

A "very talented" young student receiving cancer treatment at the Christie died from sepsis he likely contracted due to poor hand hygiene at the hospital, a coroner has concluded.

Joshua Abbott-Littler, from Wigan, had been diagnosed with a rare and acute form of leukaemia on June 8 last year. The 20-year-old had been taken to Royal Albert Edward Infirmary, in his hometown, after displaying "flu-like symptoms" with worsening chest pains, sore throat, tiredness and spontaneous bruising.

Yesterday, an inquest at Manchester Coroners' Court heard how he died in the early hours of June 26, 2025, at The Christie hospital. The court was told he contracted an infection due to cross-contamination from "inadequate adherence" to hand hygiene on the ward.

View 3 Images The coroner heard how Josh was an "intelligent" young man (Image: Liverpool Echo)

Body found in sea during huge hunt for missing man who vanished from home READ MORE :

Coroner Andrew Bridgman heard how Josh was an "intelligent" and "very talented" young man, who enjoyed music, attending gigs and festivals. He was studying an integrated masters degree in maths at Lancaster University, but received a diagnosis of Hodgkin lymphoma in 2023, reports the Manchester Evening News.

He was described as being "fit and well" in the summer after ringing the bell for the "all clear" at The Christie, Manchester in May 2024. The inquest was informed that Joshua was tragically diagnosed with treatment-induced acute myeloid leukaemia on June 8 - a rare, aggressive form of leukaemia caused by certain cancer treatments, such as chemotherapy.

He was readmitted to The Christie, but died in the early hours of June 26. The coroner determined that Joshua's death resulted from multiple organ failure due to sepsis following a bacterial infection, which he contracted through cross-contamination from another patient on the ward.

His official cause of death was listed as multiple organ failure, multidrug-resistant sepsis spread via cross-contamination from another patient on the ward, and treatment-induced acute myeloid leukaemia. However, it was noted that his Hodgkin lymphoma diagnosis did not directly contribute to his death.

In the days leading up to Joshua's passing, his condition deteriorated rapidly, resulting in multiple organ failure. An echocardiogram revealed that his heart function was severely impaired at 35 per cent, compared to the normal range of 55-60 per cent.

View 3 Images Josh's cause of death was listed as multiple organ failure (Image: Liverpool Echo)

On the evening of June 24, he was transferred to the critical care unit at The Christie, and the next day he was administered specialist antibiotics, described as "rarely used in other contexts", after testing positive for CPE (Carbapenemase-producing Enterobacterales).

Following Joshua's tragic passing, a PSII (Patient Safety Incident Investigation) report from the NHS trust revealed that genetic testing confirmed the strain of bacteria which led to Josh's admission into the critical care unit was identical to that of another patient on the ward, indicating cross-contamination.

The infection originated in Joshua's bloodstream. The report further stated that staff members must employ an aseptic non-touch technique (ANTT), designed to prevent microorganisms from hands, surfaces or equipment being introduced into a susceptible site such as an intravenous device, urinary catheter or wound - in Joshua's case, this was a PICC line (Peripherally Inserted Central Catheter).

The report suggested that poor adherence to appropriate hand hygiene and control measures including ANTT is "highly likely" to have been the cause of the infection entering Joshua's bloodstream.

Joshua exhibited signs of multiple organ failure, a result of severe sepsis. His condition rapidly deteriorated, it was irreversible, and he could not survive this. The coroner added that The Christie acknowledged cross-contamination and admitted there were lapses in infection control and prevention.

Coroner Bridgman concluded that although Joshua's death was initially attributed to his previous cancer treatment, it was ultimately "overtaken by the inadequate adherence to antiseptic techniques". He described this as one of the more "tragic inquests", labelling the circumstances of Joshua's death as "devastating" for his family and extended his condolences.

A spokesperson for The Christie NHS Foundation Trust expressed: "We offer our sincere condolences to Joshua's family.

"The safety of our patients is The Christie's highest priority, and we have stringent infection control measures in place across all our services. Following this incident, we have carried out a thorough review and have already implemented further improvements to strengthen our infection prevention practices to ensure we provide the safest possible care to all our patients.

Article continues below

"Our thoughts remain with Joshua's family at this difficult time, and we are deeply sorry for their loss." In the wake of Joshua's passing, his family raised funds for Young Lives vs Cancer by participating in a nine-hour walk from Wigan to Southport. The charity provides support to children battling cancer and their families.

News.title: Police lock down Stockport beauty spot as dead body discovered
News.content:
Police have sealed off a UK beauty spot after finding a dead body.

It is understood the deceased man was found near Marple Aqueduct in Stockport and has not yet been formally identified. A witness reported seeing a police helicopter flying overhead with the aqueduct cordoned off this morning.

Officers have been pictured gathering near a tree-lined area beside at least two parked police vehicles.

A Greater Manchester Police spokesperson said the man's next of kin had been informed. It is understood police are not treating the incident as suspicious, reports Manchester Evening News.

The Marple Aqueduct was built in the late 1700s to carry the lower level of the Peak Forest Canal across the River Goyt. The 16-lock flight is Grade II listed, and one of the steepest in Britain.

Image: Getty Images) Getty Images)

News.title: Donald Trump 'humiliated' after suffering 'brutal' blow as Iran war rages on
News.content:
Donald Trump 'humiliated' after suffering 'brutal' blow as Iran war rages on

President Donald Trump is said to have suffered a "brutal blow" as the war rages on in Iran. The situation is claimed to have left him "humiliated" with no sign of peace in sight

View Image President Donald Trump is said to have experienced a "brutal" blow (stock image) (Image: Getty)

It hasn't been a positive week for the President of the United States, as it began with him being labelled the "world's biggest joke". Now Donald Trump has been dealt yet another setback as the Iran war continues unabated.

‌



More than two weeks ago the President confirmed the US military's collaboration with Israel in an attempt to overthrow Iran's current regime, and tension in the Middle East has intensified ever since. However, it appears the President has encountered a setback this week after France delivered some particularly forceful words about its stance on the war as it shows no indication of ending any time soon.

‌



It's not the first time Trump has been dealt a blow over the past few weeks. Days ago he was also accused of telling lies about the escalating conflict.

‌



Hal, known online as Hal for NY, has now highlighted a development that's allegedly left Trump "humiliated". He stated: "France just humiliated Donald Trump as they not only declined his request to help in Iran, they poured salt in the wound.

"Now, as you know, Donald Trump has been begging our allies to come bail him out in the Strait of Hormuz, and President Macron of France was one of the first to deny that request, saying that France did not choose this Iran war.

"'We are not taking part in it, nobody can force us'. While that statement is powerful enough on its own, a French general decided to chime in, adding 'joining Trump's coalition today is like buying a ticket to dinner and dancing on the Titanic after it hit the iceberg'.

‌



"That is brutal, and is the truth of where Trump is at right now, because he later added that Donald Trump is not looking for help in the war, he is looking for somebody to share the political cost, which is exactly right.

"Donald Trump got into a situation and he can't help himself. He should just cut his losses and get out but, instead, he's hoping somebody else will take the fall for him."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



The footage has amassed thousands of views since it was posted, with numerous viewers weighing in on the matter. Some branded the President's move "mad".

One remarked: "The outcome of this is astronomical." Another added: "What a mess."

A third responded: "Trump will never admit defeat. He will die on this hill and drag everyone down with him."

‌



Meanwhile, a fourth also commented: "His pride won't let him get out. He's his own worst enemy."

On Tuesday (March 17), it emerged that a French general likened joining President Donald Trump's war to "buying a discounted ticket for the Titanic after it had already hit the iceberg". Former commander of the French Foreign Legion, Michel Yakovleff, appeared on LCI, outlining why France ought not to join Trump's war.

Throughout this week, several allies dismissed the notion of providing military backing for Trump's conflict with Iran. "You can't have an American operation where they're bombing whatever they can, and then below that, the Europeans doing something else," Yakovleff said, according to translators.

Article continues below

"No, no, no, it has to be one sole operation, under a NATO flag. I don't think he's understood that." Furthermore, Yakovleff warned that dispatching vessels to support Trump would not only place the ships in danger, but would also draw others into the political dimensions of the conflict.

"It's not a question of military means, the Strait of Hormuz," he said. "It's that he wants to share the political risk, not the military risk."

News.title: Students at Kent meningitis uni expose grim conditions from 'rats to no water'
News.content:
Students at Kent meningitis uni expose grim conditions from 'rats to no water'

As an 'unprecedented outbreak' of meningitis continues to unfold in Kent, students have made a number of grim allegations about chaos on campus, from rat infestations to no water in halls

View 10 Images Students at the University of Kent have expressed concerns regarding alleged conditions on campus

Students at the University of Kent have expressed concerns over the alleged conditions on campus amid a devastating meningitis outbreak.

‌



Two people have died following an outbreak of "invasive" meningitis in the Kent area. The university, the main campus of which is situated close to the historic Canterbury Cathedral, has since confirmed that one of the deceased is a 24-year-old Kent student. It's also understood that a further 11 students from the Canterbury area are now receiving hospital treatment, having become seriously ill with symptoms of meningitis and septicaemia.

‌



Earlier this week photographs emerged showing lengthy queues stretching across the 300-acre campus, with hundreds lining up outside the Senate building to receive meningitis antibiotics. While campuses remain open at the time of writing, the university has confirmed there will be no in-person assessments this week, "in light of the developing situation". Students have also been urged to keep an eye on their emails, so as to be aware of any updates.

‌



Now, alarming allegations have been made by Kent Uni students, which paint a picture of complete chaos as the situation unfolds, from incidents of rodents being spotted in halls to a fire breaking out at the library in the early hours of the morning.

10 View 10 Images A number of students have complained about water issues at the uni 10 View 10 Images Footage shows a student trying to turn on a tap in halls, but to no avail

‌



Highlighting a series of catastrophes likened to "the Egyptian plagues" by one commenter, one frustrated student took to TikTok to share footage of them attempting to turn on the tap in their halls of residence, but to no avail. They captioned the post: "Sorry, where is our water. First, no water, then no hot water, then [a fire], and now a meningitis outbreak."

It would appear that this water issue has previously led to complaints. Back in January, another student uploaded a TikTok video of themselves looking visibly exasperated as they twisted the tap in their student kitchen, writing, "POV: u live on campus and ur looking for ANY drop of water so u can eat, wash dishes, or even shower." On this occasion, the official University of Kent responded in the comment section, replying: "Park Wood is back up and running! Unfortunately, a number of buildings are still affected on campus, though. South East Water is working on repairs locally, and student updates can be found at student.kent.ac.uk"

As well as water, University of Kent students have reportedly also had to deal with fire. A third individual this week shared footage of fellow students making their way out of the uni library at 1 am, after a blaze reportedly broke out. Baffled followers expressed amazement that Kent students were dealing with yet another situation, with one person commenting: "Why is the world ending in Canterbury?"

‌



10 View 10 Images An alleged fire at the library forced students to evacuate 10 View 10 Images Others have complained about infestations of wasps and rats

A fourth student even went as far as to tell potential applicants "don't bother", stating "Uni of Kent has been so awful this year. - No water - rat infestation.... meningitis outbreak?! I wonder what's next." TikTok is flooded with students from the university complaining about rats on campus. Just one week ago, a woman called Amelia shared footage in which she claimed rats were scrabbling about in her kitchen cupboards.

‌



The sounds of movement are audible as Amelia exclaims, "Oh my god". In another clip, rodents can be seen scurrying outside the front of their accommodation as students scream and run away. Just a few days later, Amelia packed up her bags and got her dad to pick her up to go back home. In a now viral video, she wrote: "First we had no water, then we had rats, now dad's picking me up in the middle of the night after the meningitis outbreak".

In response to Amelia's video of rats in her kitchen, which caused shudders amongst Amelia's followers, the University of Kent issued the following reply, commenting: "Hey, thank you for bringing this to our attention. As a parkland campus, we're lucky to be surrounded by green space and natural habitats, which means we do see a range of wildlife across campus.

10 View 10 Images Footage shared via TikTok shows rats scurrying about close to accommodation (Image: TIKTOK) 10 View 10 Images The university has said reports of a rodent infestation are being 'treated as a priority" (Image: TIKTOK)

‌



"Occasionally, animals can make their way into buildings or areas where they shouldn't. We completely understand that this isn't pleasant, and we work hard to manage the campus environment and minimise situations like this. This has now been reported to our Accommodation team and is being treated as a priority. Please do continue to report anything you notice so we can address it as quickly as possible. Thanks, James"

This comes as a Change.org petition calling for the university to close amid the meningitis outbreak has gathered more than 5,000 signatures in the space of just 12 hours. Many of those who have signed the petition have expressed grave concern for their welfare and the welfare of others, with a psychology student by the name of Albert penning: "I would rather fail and resit than risk getting the infection."

Another, named Charlie, commented: "We had a water shortage.... now there is a life-threatening disease spreading that has caused the death of a fellow student and many to be infected, the campus decides its paying attendees should put their health at risk. This is not how an educational institution should be run."

‌



10 View 10 Images There have even been reports of rats scrabbling about in kitchen cupboards (Image: Cameo) 10 View 10 Images A number of students have been fleeing campus amid the unfolding situation (Image: TIKTOK)

In a bid to protect students, around 5,000 living in Kent's halls of residence will now be offered the meningitis B (menB) vaccine in the coming days, with health officials rushing to halt further spread. Hundreds of people are also being offered antibiotics as a course of immediate treatment. The total number of meningitis cases linked to Kent has risen to 20, up from the 15 reported yesterday.

‌



The UKHSA said: "Given the severity of the outbreak, and as an additional precautionary measure, a targeted vaccination programme will begin, starting with students that are residents of the Canterbury campus halls of residence at the University of Kent who will be contacted directly. Initially, it's expected that up to 5,000 students will be contacted and offered the vaccine. UKHSA will continue to assess ongoing risk to other populations and the programme may be extended."

Addressing the House of Commons yesterday (March 17), Health Secretary Wes Streeting revealed that the majority of cases can be traced back to Canterbury's Club Chemistry nightclub, and have particularly affected those who attended on March 5, 6 and 7. The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) learned of the first case on March 13, with a targeted vaccination programme announced today for those living in halls.

View 10 Images Health Secretary Wes Streeting revealed that the majority of cases can be traced back to Canterbury's Club Chemistry nightclub (Image: Tim Merry)

‌



Describing this "unprecedented outbreak" as a "rapidly developing situation", Mr Streeting warned that meningitis symptoms may be "easily mistaken for common conditions", such as a hangover. Mr Streeting emphasised that the meningitis strain associated with this particular outbreak, MenB, is "uncommon" and "potentially lethal", and can be spread through prolonged kissing and sharing vapes, He continued: "It doesn't spread very easily. The bacteria is passed to others after a long period of close contact, for example, living with someone in shared accommodation, through prolonged kissing or sharing vapes and drinks."

Meanwhile, UKHSA has contacted more than 30,000 students, their families and university staff to inform them of the outbreak, BBC News reports, with specialists now interviewing affected individuals, as well as their families, to "help identify all close contacts and arrange antibiotics to limit spread," the UKHSA said.

A University of Kent spokesperson has previously issued the following statement: "We are deeply saddened to confirm that one student from the University of Kent has died following a case of invasive meningitis. Our thoughts are with the student’s family, friends and the wider university community at this extremely difficult time. The safety of our students and staff remains our highest priority. We are working closely with public health teams and are in touch with staff and students to ensure they get the advice and support they need."

Article continues below

The Mirror has reached out to the University of Kent for comment.

Do you have a story to share? Email me at julia.banim@reachplc.com

News.title: Morocco awarded Africa Cup of Nations title after Senegal win overturned
News.content:
Morocco have been declared the winners of the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations after the Confederation of African Football (Caf) overturned the result of the final following Senegal's controversial walk-off.

Senegal beat Morocco 1-0 in the final on 18 January in a match that was overshadowed when the Senegalese players refused to play after the hosts were awarded a stoppage-time penalty with the match goalless.

Following a delay of about 17 minutes, the players did eventually return and Brahim Diaz's penalty was saved before Senegal's Pape Gueye scored an extra-time winner.

However, that result has now been overturned after a decision by an appeal board at African football's governing body.

A statement from Caf said Senegal had been "declared to have forfeited the final match" with the "result of the match being recorded as 3-0 in favour" of Morocco.

The Senegalese Football Federation said it would appeal against Caf's decision at the Court of Arbitration for Sport, calling it "an unfair, unprecedented and unacceptable decision which brings discredit to African football".

The decision follows an appeal by the Moroccan FA (FRMF) which Caf said was "declared admissible in form" and was upheld.

News.title: Angela Rayner's explosive speech reignites leadership speculation
News.content: Some Labour MPs have privately expressed surprise that Rayner had chosen to set herself against the government's planned restrictions on indefinite leave to remain, which gives a person the right to live, work and study in the UK for as long as they like and apply for benefits if they are eligible.

News.title: Assisted dying 'is not going away' says MSP behind rejected bill
News.content: "This issue is not going away, the number of people affected by these bad deaths - given the demographic trends, given the advances in medicine - those numbers are only going to increase, and meantime the overwhelming majority of people across Scotland are supportive of a change."

News.title: Ukraine faces missile shortage due to Middle East war, says Zelensky
News.content: "The regimes in Russia and Iran are brothers in hatred and that is why they are brothers in weapons," Zelensky said. "And we want regimes built on hatred, to never, never win in anything. And we want no such regime to threaten Europe or our partners."

News.title: Total repression and air strikes bring unrelenting dread for Iranians
News.content: "The situation is frightening…," says Ali. "The skies of your country are controlled by enemy forces. But at the same time there is always a hope in people's hearts. It's not that we are supporting America or Israel. But hoping simply that for one moment, something might happen that ends the current Iranian regime, and that the people will be able to create change."

News.title: Ad for AI editing app which said it could 'remove anything' banned
News.content: It is not clear whether the image in the ad is of a real person or is itself AI-generated, with the ASA telling the BBC making such an assessment had not been part of its investigation.

News.title: Adoption system leaves too many parents isolated, government admits after BBC investigation
News.content: "For some adopters that might mean they decide not to go through the process, but I think many still would - and they would do that with their eyes wide open to the history and the experiences that these kids have had before they come into their family."

News.title: Temperatures forecast to reach 20C in warmest day of the year so far
News.content:
Colder Arctic air across the UK in the last week meant temperatures struggled to reach double figures for many.

Combined with some wintry showers and snow over higher ground, the weekend felt like winter had made a return.

However, through this week a warmer southerly wind will draw up air from the Mediterranean and bring temperatures around seven or eight degrees above average on Wednesday.

Widely across England and Wales temperatures will get into the mid to high teens.

For Wales we're very likely to see the warmest day of the year so far with the temperature exceeding the 17.8C recorded at Porthmadog on 5 March.

There's a possibility somewhere in west or north-west Wales could see a temperature exceeding 20C for the first time this year.

That would surpass the UK's current highest temperature of the year so far of 19.2C - set in London earlier in March.

Southern and eastern coastal locations in England will be a few degrees lower as the air will be blowing over a relatively cooler sea surface.

Despite some warmth by day, be prepared for some fairly chilly and foggy nights later this week.

Boxing.title: Jake Paul teases boxing return just three months after having his jaw broken by Anthony Joshua
Boxing.content:
Jake Paul teases boxing return just three months after having his jaw broken by Anthony Joshua



Jake Paul appears set to announce his return to the ring just weeks after undergoing a second surgery on the broken jaw he suffered against Anthony Joshua.

The YouTuber-turned-boxer, 29, had his jaw shattered in two places by Joshua back in December after stepping up to face the former unified heavyweight champion in a dangerous mismatch which sparked fierce controversy.

A lethal right hand floored Paul to the point of no return in the sixth round of the contentious Miami bout, while also leaving him with a sickening injury which has cast doubt over his fighting career.

After having an initial op almost immediately, he was forced to go under the knife for a second time last month as the screws and plates in his jaw were 'coming loose'.

Yet nearly four weeks later, Paul has teased an announcement over his potential boxing return on Wednesday.

The online sensation shared a video of himself hitting the pads and wrote on Monday: 'You won't expect this one hahahaha - announcement coming in 2 days March 18th'.

Jake Paul appears set to announce his boxing return despite undergoing a second jaw surgery

The YouTuber-turned-boxer had his jaw shattered by Anthony Joshua back in December

You won't expect this one hahahaha 😈⚡️announcement coming in 2 days March 18th pic.twitter.com/NT20egDt0p — Jake Paul (@jakepaul) March 16, 2026

At the start of February, Paul's Most Valuable Promotions co-founder Nakisa Bidarian said he is targeting a return to the ring in 2026 - and a move back down to cruiserweight - following his defeat to Joshua.

He drafted in heavyweight AJ at short notice, having initially been preparing for the significantly shorter and lighter Gervonta Davis, who was pulled out of their scheduled bout amid allegations of domestic abuse.

It remains to be seen which opponent Paul chooses next, with a rematch against bitter rival Tommy Fury potentially an option.

Fury, the younger brother of Joshua's heavyweight nemesis Tyson, handed Paul his only other professional defeat when they met in 2023.

Nevertheless, he may not be able to begin sparring again until later this year, meaning a return could come at the end of 2026.

Asked when he expects to fight again, Paul said earlier this month: 'Later this year. I got my second surgery recently.



'So the doctors said like four, five, or six months to even be able to spar to see how the bone is healing.'

Boxing.title: Touching footage shows Anthony Joshua dancing with mums of his two friends who died - after heartfelt speech during Mother's Day party
Boxing.content:
Anthony Joshua danced with the mothers of his two late friends during touching scenes at a Mother's Day party.

At the celebration on Sunday, AJ was filmed dancing with the mums of Sina Ghami and Latif 'Latz' Ayodele.

Sina and 'Latz' were killed last December in a horrendous car accident in Nigeria, while AJ and the driver survived.

Their deaths have had a profound impact on the boxer but he has been seen regularly with their family members and acquitted himself admirably in the aftermath of the tragedy.

Footage of Sunday's Mother's Day celebration shows AJ making a short speech to the assembled guests.

He said: 'To come under one roof and celebrate our mothers, celebrate our brothers, celebrate those that are not with us, and celebrate those that are with us.

Anthony Joshua was seen dancing with the mothers of his two friends who were killed

'Knowing that all of our fates will one day come, we just stay strong until that day. We pray for our transition and just to be great in the afterlife.

'And why I talk about that is because my heart’s for my bros right now, do you know what I mean?

'So, thank you again; why we’ve done this is obviously for my mum and our family, but also for Sina, Latz, Sina’s mum, and Latz’s mum.

'It’s all interconnected and I’m not making too much sense — I’m just speaking from my heart, basically.

'So thank you, mums, for giving me Latz and Sina for so many years and for looking over me, you know? I appreciate it a lot. Thank you very much.'

Joshua shared a number of clips from the event on his Snapchat story and appeared to be in high spirits.

It has been a hugely difficult few months for the former heavyweight champion, who was lucky to escape last year's car wreck with his life, miraculously walking away with minor injuries.

The 36-year-old has paid emotional tributes to his friends on social media and vowed to take care of their families in the aftermath, while he also had their names tattooed on his arm.

Joshua, born in Watford to Nigerian parents, was on holiday in Lagos after his win over Jake Paul in Miami on December 19. In the days after the crash Joshua thanked fans for the support they had shown after the tragedy.

Joshua pictured with Latif Ayodele (left) and Sina Ghami (right)

AJ has dealt with the deaths of his two close friends admirably and been a pillar of strength

In January, AJ spoke for the first time at length about the tragedy with an emotional video on Instagram.
